question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user with local shell access create /tmp/ready themselves (since /tmp is world-writable and os.Stat('/tmp/ready') performs no ownership check), causing the readiness probe to return exit 0 and the orchestrator to route traffic to an unready pod?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user create /tmp/alive with a freshly-set mtime (e.g., via touch /tmp/alive) before the real pinger process writes it, causing the liveness probe to return exit 0 even when the pinger goroutine has never run, masking a dead process from the orchestrator?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user place a symlink at /tmp/ready pointing to any world-readable file (e.g., /etc/hostname), causing os.Stat('/tmp/ready') to succeed and return exit 0, spoofing readiness without the pinger ever having initialized?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user place a symlink at /tmp/alive pointing to any world-readable file with a recent mtime (e.g., /proc/self/status), causing the liveness check to pass and time.Since(fi.ModTime()) to be under 2*time.Minute, masking a dead pinger?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user delete /tmp/ready (if they own it or /tmp is sticky-bit but they created the file) to force os.Stat('/tmp/ready') to return an error, causing the readiness probe to return exit 1 and the orchestrator to stop routing traffic, achieving a denial-of-service?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user delete /tmp/alive to force os.Stat('/tmp/alive') to fail, causing the liveness probe to return exit 1 and the orchestrator to restart the container, achieving a denial-of-service via repeated container restarts?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user use touch -t to set the mtime of /tmp/alive to a time more than 2 minutes in the past, causing time.Since(fi.ModTime()) > 2*time.Minute and the liveness probe to return exit 1, triggering a container restart without any actual failure?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user use the utimes() syscall to set the mtime of /tmp/alive to a future timestamp, causing time.Since(fi.ModTime()) to return a negative duration that is never > 2*time.Minute, permanently bypassing the staleness check and keeping a dead pinger appearing alive?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user create /tmp/alive with a future mtime (e.g., year 2099) so that time.Since(fi.ModTime()) is always a large negative value, meaning the age > 2*time.Minute condition is never true, permanently spoofing liveness regardless of pinger state?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user replace /tmp/alive with a directory named 'alive', causing os.Stat('/tmp/alive') to succeed (directories have mtimes) and the liveness check to pass based on the directory's mtime, bypassing the intent of the heartbeat file check?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user replace /tmp/ready with a directory named 'ready', causing os.Stat('/tmp/ready') to succeed and the readiness probe to return exit 0, spoofing readiness without the pinger ever writing the file?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user create a hard link at /tmp/alive pointing to a file with a controlled mtime, causing the liveness check to pass since os.Stat() follows hard links and returns the target's mtime, bypassing the heartbeat mechanism?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user exploit the TOCTOU window between os.Stat('/tmp/alive') returning success and the process exiting with 0, by swapping /tmp/alive with a stale file after the stat but before the exit, to cause inconsistent probe results under concurrent execution?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user pass an unexpected value as os.Args[1] (e.g., a very long string or a string with special characters) to trigger the default case, causing the healthcheck to exit with code 2, which some orchestrators may interpret as a probe failure and restart the container?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user pass os.Args[1] as an empty string '' to trigger the default case (since '' != 'ready' and '' != 'live'), causing exit code 2 and a potential container restart or probe failure depending on orchestrator configuration?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user pass os.Args[1] as 'Ready' (capital R) or 'LIVE' (uppercase) to trigger the default case due to case-sensitive string comparison in the switch statement, causing exit code 2 and a probe failure?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user pass multiple arguments (e.g., os.Args = ['healthcheck', 'ready', 'extra']) where only os.Args[1] is used, and craft os.Args[1] to be 'ready' while injecting a second argument that is logged or processed elsewhere, causing unexpected behavior?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user fill the /tmp filesystem (e.g., by writing large files to /tmp) to prevent the pinger's os.WriteFile('/tmp/alive', ...) from succeeding, causing the mtime of /tmp/alive to become stale, triggering a false liveness failure and container restart?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user fill the /tmp filesystem to prevent the pinger's os.WriteFile('/tmp/ready', ...) from succeeding, causing the readiness probe to permanently return exit 1 and the pod to never receive traffic, achieving a persistent denial-of-service?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user exhaust the inode count on the /tmp filesystem by creating many small files, preventing os.WriteFile('/tmp/alive') from creating new inodes, causing the heartbeat to stop updating and the liveness probe to eventually fail?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user create a named pipe (FIFO) at /tmp/ready, causing os.Stat('/tmp/ready') to succeed (FIFOs have stat entries) and the readiness probe to return exit 0, spoofing readiness without the pinger ever writing the file?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user create a named pipe (FIFO) at /tmp/alive with a recent mtime, causing os.Stat('/tmp/alive') to succeed and time.Since(fi.ModTime()) to be under 2*time.Minute, spoofing liveness without the pinger heartbeat goroutine running?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user create a Unix domain socket file at /tmp/ready, causing os.Stat('/tmp/ready') to succeed and the readiness probe to return exit 0, since os.Stat() does not distinguish file types and performs no content or type validation?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user create a Unix domain socket file at /tmp/alive with a recent mtime, causing the liveness probe to pass since os.Stat() returns mtime for socket files and time.Since(fi.ModTime()) will be under 2*time.Minute?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: '] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged local user exploit the lack of file content validation in the 'ready' case (os.Stat only checks existence, not content) to create /tmp/ready with arbitrary content (e.g., malicious bytes), causing the readiness probe to pass while the file signals nothing meaningful?
